family_audit: accepted_primary: active PTX hot-band control/register surface on the 128x128 microkernel
accepted_secondary: register-pressure reduction via PTX helper flattening
accepted_restore: historical PTX grouping-window restore to widen live family coverage
deferred: grouped CTA traversal/locality on the PTX grid after the round-4 PASS_LOSS regression
rejected_primary: the 256x128 pivot promotion family remains negative for this loop

directions.name: Continue The Active PTX One-K 128x128 Control-Path Exploit
directions.hypothesis: The grouped-CTA traversal retune just lost 0.341504 ms versus the 24.177664 ms accepted PTX base, which is a strong signal that CTA ordering is not the next high-leverage knob on this branch. The active kernel is still the PTX 128x128 hot-band microkernel at 200 registers per thread with only 16.60% active warps and 48.36% tensor-pipe activity, while DRAM remains low at 9.73%. The next best move is therefore to stay on the winning PTX branch and retune the inner control/export surface that actually governs issue efficiency: copy/consume order, helper lifetime, and export sequencing inside the live microkernel, not another grid-traversal experiment.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual PTX hot-band control-path overhead and live-range pressure inside the current 128x128 microkernel, not global bandwidth or another CTA-ordering miss.
directions.risk: Moderate. This stays on the best measured family and touches the actual hot path, but PTX control-surface tweaks can still collapse into noise or silently grow live ranges if the scope is not bounded.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.177664 ms base
ranking_notes: Round 4 already falsified grouped-CTA traversal as the primary next family on top of the current PTX winner.
This is the most coherent path that keeps the best-performing branch alive while targeting the still register-limited hot kernel.
It remains ahead of the historical restores because it attacks the live branch directly instead of rolling back to an older near-best state.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if another bounded PTX-local pass also leaves register count, active warps, and hot-band time effectively unchanged or loses cleanly against the 24.177664 ms base.

directions.name: Flatten PTX Hot-Band Compute Helpers To Reduce Register Pressure
directions.hypothesis: The live PTX branch is still pinned at 200 registers per thread and only 2 resident CTAs per SM, so occupancy and latency hiding remain constrained even when the traversal mapping changes. A historically promising family at 24.696832 ms was to flatten the PTX hot-band accumulate/helper surface so ptxas can shorten live ranges, remove unnecessary helper abstraction, and maybe recover a few warps of residency without touching macro geometry. That makes register-pressure reduction the strongest non-restore secondary family now that the traversal probe has failed.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Register-limited occupancy and weak latency hiding driven by helper structure and live-range expansion in the PTX hot-band compute path.
directions.risk: Medium. The change is still local to the PTX microkernel, but helper flattening can easily become a no-op if the generated code and live ranges do not actually change.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.177664 ms base
ranking_notes: The register-limited signature stayed intact through the traversal regression, so this family remains well-supported by the current profile.
It is ranked below dir_01 because the direct control-path exploit is on the exact best branch today.
The historical rehydration keeps this family live even though it was displaced by the recent policy change.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if register count does not move down materially or if occupancy remains stuck at the same ceiling after one bounded helper-flattening pass.

directions.name: Restore The Best Measured PTX Grouping Window On The Accepted Surface
directions.hypothesis: The just-measured grouped_rows=8 traversal retune lost, but the round-history search still contains a historically near-best PTX grouping-window restore at 24.444416 ms that was measured on a stronger accepted surface than the current ad-hoc traversal probe. Keeping that older locality restore alive is useful because it widens family coverage after the policy update and preserves a clean A/B fallback if the live PTX exploit path becomes noisy. This is not the primary bet, but it is the best restore-style candidate still close enough to the current best to deserve a live slot.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Inter-CTA locality and launch-order mapping on the accepted PTX surface, but only as a historical restore fallback rather than the primary bottleneck attack.
directions.risk: Low to medium. This is a restore family with prior measured evidence, but it still reopens a locality axis that the latest round just weakened.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.177664 ms base and the historical 24.444416 ms restore run
ranking_notes: This is intentionally a restore/fallback family, not the recommended move, because the fresh grouped traversal probe just lost.
It stays ranked because the historical run is still close to the best measured PTX base and the user explicitly asked to repopulate live search families from round_history.
If the direct PTX exploit and register-pressure branch both stall, this family keeps a credible older locality window available for selection.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the restored grouping window still cannot recover to its historical near-best behavior or if it behaves like the latest traversal probe with no meaningful locality signal.

notes: Round 5/20 audit: round 4's grouped-CTA traversal probe regressed from 24.177664 ms to 24.519168 ms, while the active PTX hot-band kernel still shows the same basic signature: 200 registers/thread, register-limited occupancy at 2 CTAs per SM, only 16.60% active warps, 48.36% tensor-pipe activity, and very low DRAM pressure. That rejects CTA traversal/locality as the primary family for the next round and puts the focus back on the PTX microkernel's control-path and live-range pressure. Because the recent search-policy update thinned the live queue, the ranking also pulls in two historically strong families from round_history: a register-pressure/helper-flattening branch and a restore of the best measured PTX grouping window. The 256x128 pivot family stays deferred after its earlier loss.
